url: local://incident_cards/Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL 17.4 Jepsen analysis: multi-AZ reader endpoint violates snapshot isolation
document_title: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL 17.4 Jepsen analysis: multi-AZ reader endpoint violates snapshot isolation
text:
Title: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL 17.4 Jepsen analysis: multi-AZ reader endpoint violates snapshot isolation
Summary: Jepsen found that Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL multi-AZ clusters violate Snapshot Isolation across the primary and reader endpoints even under healthy conditions and without failover. The issue appears as Long Fork and other G-nonadjacent cycles, likely because primaries and secondaries disagree on transaction visibility order.
Canonical symptoms: inconsistent_reads_between_primary_and_reader; snapshot_isolation_violation; long_fork; healthy_cluster_anomaly
Affected components: primary_endpoint; reader_endpoint; transaction_visibility_order; wal_replication
Failure mode candidates: reader_endpoint_visibility_divergence; long_fork; primary_vs_secondary_order_disagreement
Diagnostic patterns: healthy managed cluster -> writes succeed normally -> read-only queries on secondaries disagree about order of committed writes -> inconsistent snapshot appears without obvious outage
Root cause summary: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL multi-AZ clusters appear to violate Snapshot Isolation across endpoints because primaries and secondaries can expose different transaction visibility orders, producing Long Fork and related anomalies.
Violated properties: Snapshot Isolation across primary and reader endpoints
Claimed guarantees: Snapshot Isolation as strongest level supported across all endpoints
Mitigations or workarounds: Use the writer endpoint for safety-critical reads.; Avoid assuming reader endpoints preserve the same snapshot semantics as the primary.; Design invariants with Long Fork risk in mind when using secondaries.

url: local://incident_cards/CockroachDB beta-20160829 Jepsen analysis: serializability violations and stale-read surprises
document_title: CockroachDB beta-20160829 Jepsen analysis: serializability violations and stale-read surprises
text:
Title: CockroachDB beta-20160829 Jepsen analysis: serializability violations and stale-read surprises
Summary: Jepsen found multiple serializability and ordering anomalies in early CockroachDB betas, despite strong consistency messaging around stale reads and majority-replica freshness. Fixes were included in later beta releases, but the report highlights practical limits around clock assumptions and database-wide external consistency.
Canonical symptoms: serializability_violation; stale_or_out_of_order_reads; strict_serializability_gap; clock_sensitivity
Affected components: transaction_protocol; hybrid_logical_clocks; multi_range_transactions; leaseholder_reads
Failure mode candidates: strict_serializability_violation; double_applied_transaction; timestamp_or_clock_offset_issue; database_wide_external_consistency_gap
Diagnostic patterns: mostly healthy system under bounded clocks -> corner-case transaction ordering tests reveal stale or out-of-order visibility
Root cause summary: Early CockroachDB betas had multiple bugs and design-limit interactions around timestamping, clock bounds, and transaction ordering, producing serializability and strict-ordering anomalies that later betas partially fixed.
Violated properties: strict serializability / external consistency; some serializability-related expectations in tested betas
Claimed guarantees: serializability; no stale reads
Mitigations or workarounds: Keep clock offsets tightly bounded.; Use versions that include fixes from beta-20160915 and beta-20161013.; Avoid assuming full external consistency from majority-replication messaging alone.

url: local://incident_cards/etcd 3.4.3 Jepsen analysis: unsafe locks despite strong key-value guarantees
document_title: etcd 3.4.3 Jepsen analysis: unsafe locks despite strong key-value guarantees
text:
Title: etcd 3.4.3 Jepsen analysis: unsafe locks despite strong key-value guarantees
Summary: Jepsen found that etcd 3.4.3 key-value operations appeared strict serializable by default, and watches preserved ordered delivery, but distributed locks were fundamentally unsafe. The main practical risk was not ordinary stale reads in the key-value API, but using etcd locks as if they guaranteed exclusive ownership for external critical sections.
Canonical symptoms: stale_reads; lost_updates; unsafe_locking; duplicate_lock_holders; coordination_anomalies
Affected components: lock_service; lease_validation; watch_api; key_value_api; external_critical_sections
Failure mode candidates: lock_ownership_violation; stale_lock_after_lease_change; missing_lease_validity_recheck; coordination_primitive_unsound_for_external_state
Diagnostic patterns: coordination store appears healthy for basic reads/writes -> application still sees conflicting critical-section behavior -> root issue is unsafe lock usage rather than kv inconsistency; lease-bound lock waits -> lease changes or expires -> client continues as if lock ownership remained valid
Root cause summary: etcd 3.4.3 key-value operations appeared strong, but etcd locks were unsafe as a general mutual exclusion primitive, and a bug that skipped lease-validity checks after waiting for a lock made those failures worse.
Violated properties: safe mutual exclusion for distributed locks; exclusive ownership semantics for external critical sections
Claimed guarantees: strict-serializable reads, writes, and micro-transactions across the datastore; distributed locking support as part of coordination primitives; ordered watches
Mitigations or workarounds: Do not assume etcd locks safely protect arbitrary external resources.; Prefer etcd transactional guards when the protected state also lives in etcd.; Audit lock users for lease expiry, delayed keepalive, and post-wait lock ownership assumptions.; Treat etcd lock ownership as a risky coordination signal unless safety is independently verified.

url: local://incident_cards/MongoDB 4.2.6 Jepsen analysis: transactional anomalies, weak defaults, and snapshot-isolation gaps
document_title: MongoDB 4.2.6 Jepsen analysis: transactional anomalies, weak defaults, and snapshot-isolation gaps
text:
Title: MongoDB 4.2.6 Jepsen analysis: transactional anomalies, weak defaults, and snapshot-isolation gaps
Summary: Jepsen found that MongoDB 4.2.6 could violate snapshot-isolation expectations even at strong read and write concern settings, while weak defaults made behavior much worse by allowing lost writes, dirty reads, and downgraded safety in transactional contexts. The core practical danger was that users could believe they were running safe ACID-style transactions while key safety assumptions silently depended on explicit per-transaction settings.
Canonical symptoms: lost_writes; dirty_reads; read_skew; duplicate_writes; cyclic_information_flow; snapshot_isolation_violation; transaction_retry_anomalies; downgraded_transaction_safety
Affected components: transaction_api; read_concern; write_concern; session_defaults; retry_mechanism; sharded_or_replicated_cluster_behavior
Failure mode candidates: weak_default_read_write_concerns; transaction_level_settings_override_safer_handles; snapshot_read_without_majority_write; transaction_retry_bug; network_partition_exposes_transactional_anomalies
Diagnostic patterns: healthy cluster with strong-looking baseline -> introduce transactions -> safety unexpectedly weakens because transaction-level settings differ from assumed defaults; transaction workload + network partition or retry/timeout -> lost writes, duplicate effects, or snapshot anomalies; read concern snapshot without majority write concern -> apparent snapshot semantics fail even for read-only flows
Root cause summary: MongoDB 4.2.6 transactional safety was undermined by a combination of weak defaults, surprising transaction-level concern semantics, snapshot behavior that required write concern majority even for some read-only expectations, and at least one likely retry-related bug exposed under faults.
Violated properties: snapshot isolation; fully isolated transactional behavior; durability of acknowledged writes under weak or surprising concern configurations
Claimed guarantees: full ACID transactions; strong data consistency and safety guarantees; snapshot-style transactional semantics
Mitigations or workarounds: Set read and write concern explicitly on each transaction that requires strong safety.; Do not assume database- or collection-level concern settings automatically carry into transactional contexts.; Treat read concern snapshot as insufficient unless the necessary write concern guarantees are also present.; Audit retry handling and ambiguous transaction error paths carefully.

url: local://incident_cards/MySQL 8.0.34 Jepsen analysis: Repeatable Read anomalies and internal consistency surprises
document_title: MySQL 8.0.34 Jepsen analysis: Repeatable Read anomalies and internal consistency surprises
text:
Title: MySQL 8.0.34 Jepsen analysis: Repeatable Read anomalies and internal consistency surprises
Summary: Jepsen confirmed that MySQL Repeatable Read still allows anomalies including G2-item, G-single, lost update, internal consistency violations, and Monotonic Atomic View failures. The report also notes that AWS RDS MySQL clusters routinely violate Serializability.
Canonical symptoms: repeatable_read_anomaly; lost_update; read_skew; internal_consistency_violation; monotonic_atomic_view_violation
Affected components: repeatable_read_isolation_level; interactive_transactions; innodb_concurrency_control
Failure mode candidates: weaker_than_expected_repeatable_read; lost_update_under_repeatable_read; partial_visibility_of_transaction_effects
Diagnostic patterns: application relies on Repeatable Read for invariant preservation -> concurrent updates and reads still expose partial or contradictory transaction effects
Root cause summary: MySQL 8.0.34's Repeatable Read remains weaker than many users infer from the name and permits multiple transaction anomalies, including partial and internally inconsistent visibility patterns.
Violated properties: commonly expected Repeatable Read protections; Monotonic Atomic View
Claimed guarantees: Repeatable Read
Mitigations or workarounds: Do not assume Repeatable Read prevents lost update or all read skew anomalies.; Use stronger mechanisms for safety-critical invariants.

url: local://incident_cards/PostgreSQL 12.3 Jepsen analysis: serializable mode admits G2-item and repeatable read is snapshot isolation
document_title: PostgreSQL 12.3 Jepsen analysis: serializable mode admits G2-item and repeatable read is snapshot isolation
text:
Title: PostgreSQL 12.3 Jepsen analysis: serializable mode admits G2-item and repeatable read is snapshot isolation
Summary: Jepsen found that PostgreSQL 12.3 could exhibit G2-item even under serializable isolation during normal operation, while repeatable read behaved as snapshot isolation and therefore allowed anomalies users might not expect from the name.
Canonical symptoms: serializable_mode_violation; g2_item; repeatable_read_surprise
Affected components: serializable_isolation; repeatable_read_isolation; ssi_implementation
Failure mode candidates: serializable_bug; snapshot_isolation_under_repeatable_read
Diagnostic patterns: healthy single-node database -> concurrency test under named strong isolation level -> anti-dependency cycle appears
Root cause summary: PostgreSQL 12.3 had a concrete serializable-mode bug allowing G2-item, while repeatable read remained snapshot isolation and therefore weaker than some users or formalizations might expect.
Violated properties: serializability in serializable mode
Claimed guarantees: serializable execution in serializable mode; repeatable read naming suggests stronger protection than snapshot isolation
Mitigations or workarounds: Upgrade to a version including the serializable bug fix.; Do not interpret repeatable read as stronger than PostgreSQL actually documents and implements.

url: local://incident_cards/RavenDB 6.0.2 Jepsen analysis: lost updates by default and fractured reads in transaction modes
document_title: RavenDB 6.0.2 Jepsen analysis: lost updates by default and fractured reads in transaction modes
text:
Title: RavenDB 6.0.2 Jepsen analysis: lost updates by default and fractured reads in transaction modes
Summary: Jepsen found surprising transactional behavior in RavenDB 6.0.2 even on healthy single-node clusters. Transactions lost updates by default, and both optimistic concurrency and cluster-wide transaction modes allowed fractured read, undermining claims around ACID and snapshot-style isolation.
Canonical symptoms: lost_updates_by_default; fractured_read; transaction_semantics_surprise; healthy_single_node_anomaly
Affected components: session_transaction_api; optimistic_concurrency_mode; cluster_wide_transactions; replication_and_conflict_resolution
Failure mode candidates: lack_of_true_interactive_transactions; fractured_read; last_write_wins_conflict_exposure; acquire_strong_consistency_only_in_narrow_modes
Diagnostic patterns: application relies on RavenDB session semantics as ACID transaction -> healthy system still loses updates or returns a fractured view across reads
Root cause summary: RavenDB 6.0.2 exposed weaker-than-expected transaction semantics across multiple modes, including lost updates by default and fractured reads even in stronger transaction paths.
Violated properties: lost-update prevention by default; snapshot-consistent reads across transaction modes
Claimed guarantees: ACID transactions; Snapshot Isolation by default
Mitigations or workarounds: Audit which transaction mode each workflow actually uses.; Do not assume default sessions provide ACID-style isolation.; Test fractured-read-sensitive workflows directly.

url: local://incident_cards/Redis-Raft 1b3fbf6 Jepsen analysis: many safety failures in early Raft-backed Redis
document_title: Redis-Raft 1b3fbf6 Jepsen analysis: many safety failures in early Raft-backed Redis
text:
Title: Redis-Raft 1b3fbf6 Jepsen analysis: many safety failures in early Raft-backed Redis
Summary: Jepsen found twenty-one issues in early Redis-Raft builds, including partial unavailability in healthy clusters, stale reads, aborted reads, split-brain with lost updates, crashes, infinite loops, and total data loss on failover. Most issues were addressed in later development builds, but the report shows a system that was far from production safety at that stage.
Canonical symptoms: partial_unavailability; stale_reads; aborted_reads; split_brain; lost_updates; data_loss_on_failover; crash_loop; infinite_write_loop
Affected components: raft_interception_logic; leader_proxying; failover_behavior; transaction_and_read_paths
Failure mode candidates: reentrancy_bug; split_brain_under_failover; unsafe_recovery_path; immature_raft_integration
Diagnostic patterns: feature intended to add strong consistency -> ordinary operation or failover still yields stale reads, split-brain, or total write loss due to immature implementation paths
Root cause summary: Early Redis-Raft builds combined multiple severe implementation defects across proxying, failover, and recovery, leading to behaviors far weaker than the intended strict-serializable design.
Violated properties: safety during failover; basic correctness in some healthy-cluster modes
Claimed guarantees: strict serializability via Raft-backed Redis
Mitigations or workarounds: Avoid production reliance on the tested development build.; Disable or carefully validate proxying and failover paths.; Use later builds that fixed most identified issues.

url: local://incident_cards/Redpanda 21.10.1 Jepsen analysis: mixed safety and liveness failures in streaming and transactions
document_title: Redpanda 21.10.1 Jepsen analysis: mixed safety and liveness failures in streaming and transactions
text:
Title: Redpanda 21.10.1 Jepsen analysis: mixed safety and liveness failures in streaming and transactions
Summary: Jepsen found multiple Redpanda safety and liveness issues, including duplicate writes, aborted reads, inconsistent offsets, lost or stale messages, crashes, and transactional anomalies. Several issues were fixed across later releases, but some problems, especially around lost/stale messages and one crash, remained unresolved or under investigation at the time of the report.
Canonical symptoms: duplicate_messages; lost_messages; stale_messages; aborted_reads; inconsistent_offsets; transactional_anomalies; crash_during_reconfiguration; partial_unavailability
Affected components: producer_path; consumer_offsets; transaction_protocol; partition_leadership; internal_metadata_topics; idempotence_support
Failure mode candidates: duplicate_write_without_idempotence; transaction_protocol_bug; leadership_change_causes_lost_writes; offset_commit_non_monotonicity; insufficient_internal_replication_defaults
Diagnostic patterns: normal produce/consume flow -> pause/crash/partition -> duplicate or missing messages appear after recovery; transaction-enabled workload -> read_committed assumptions -> aborted reads or lost transactional writes; consumer restart or recovery -> committed offset appears authoritative -> offset moves backward or skips state
Root cause summary: Redpanda 21.10.x combined multiple distinct reliability risks: default duplication risk without idempotence, transaction-protocol bugs and weak transactional semantics, offset-state anomalies, and unresolved lost/stale message behavior during failure recovery or leadership change.
Violated properties: message durability under some failure/recovery conditions; duplicate-free processing under defaults; intuitive transactional isolation; stable monotonic committed offset expectations
Claimed guarantees: Kafka-compatible distributed streaming; safety-oriented durability claims including majority fsync for stronger acknowledgements; transaction support and exactly-once-style expectations in some workflows
Mitigations or workarounds: Enable idempotence where producer semantics depend on avoiding duplicates.; Review and harden replication settings for internal and transaction-related topics.; Treat exactly-once and transaction guarantees as configuration- and workflow-sensitive, not automatic.; Correlate recovery incidents with leader changes, retries, and committed offset behavior.

url: local://incident_cards/Scylla 4.2-rc3 Jepsen analysis: LWT split-brain and weaker-than-claimed non-LWT isolation
document_title: Scylla 4.2-rc3 Jepsen analysis: LWT split-brain and weaker-than-claimed non-LWT isolation
text:
Title: Scylla 4.2-rc3 Jepsen analysis: LWT split-brain and weaker-than-claimed non-LWT isolation
Summary: Jepsen found that both LWT and non-LWT operations in Scylla 4.2-rc3 failed to meet claimed guarantees. LWT exhibited split-brain even in healthy clusters, while non-LWT operations were not isolated as documented. Several issues were fixed in 4.2, 4.2.1, and 4.3.rc1, but split-brain risk remained around membership changes plus faults.
Canonical symptoms: lwt_split_brain; aborted_reads; missing_rows_in_batch_results; non_lwt_isolation_gap; membership_change_risk
Affected components: lightweight_transactions; membership_changes; non_lwt_write_path; batch_statements
Failure mode candidates: lwt_split_brain; membership_change_concurrency_issue; aborted_read_with_lwt; weaker_than_claimed_non_lwt_isolation
Diagnostic patterns: application relies on LWT for strong coordination -> healthy cluster or membership change still allows split-brain-like outcomes
Root cause summary: Scylla 4.2-rc3 had multiple gaps between claimed and actual isolation behavior, especially around LWT split-brain, with additional instability introduced by membership changes and certain batch/read paths.
Violated properties: single-winner / linearizable expectations for LWT; claimed isolation for non-LWT operations
Claimed guarantees: isolated writes within a partition; stronger LWT semantics
Mitigations or workarounds: Upgrade past 4.2, 4.2.1, and 4.3.rc1 issue lines where applicable.; Treat membership changes as a high-risk operational window for LWT-sensitive workloads.; Do not assume non-LWT operations are isolated.

url: local://incident_cards/VoltDB 6.3 Jepsen analysis: stale reads, dirty reads, and lost updates despite strict-serializable claims
document_title: VoltDB 6.3 Jepsen analysis: stale reads, dirty reads, and lost updates despite strict-serializable claims
text:
Title: VoltDB 6.3 Jepsen analysis: stale reads, dirty reads, and lost updates despite strict-serializable claims
Summary: Jepsen found that VoltDB 6.3, despite strong strict-serializability claims, exhibited stale reads, dirty reads, and lost updates due to internal optimizations and bugs. Fixes were made available in version 6.4.
Canonical symptoms: stale_reads; dirty_reads; lost_updates; strict_serializability_claim_gap
Affected components: single_partition_initiator; read_path_bypassing_spi; multipartition_execution
Failure mode candidates: read_path_optimization_breaks_real_time_order; strict_serializability_gap; lost_update_under_buggy_execution
Diagnostic patterns: system claims strict serializability by default -> read or update path optimization bypasses ordering mechanism -> stale or dirty observations appear
Root cause summary: VoltDB 6.3 had implementation and optimization issues that broke the stronger real-time guarantees implied by its strict-serializable positioning, leading to stale reads, dirty reads, and lost updates.
Violated properties: strict serializability
Claimed guarantees: strict serializability
Mitigations or workarounds: Upgrade to 6.4 or later.; Test read-after-write guarantees explicitly in production-like workloads.
